title: Peritoneal Dialysis Care
subtitle: The nurse performs a peritoneal dialysis exchange safely — warming the dialysate, using sterile technique, timing inflow/dwell/outflow, recognizing cloudy effluent as peritonitis, and troubleshooting poor drainage.

patient.name: Peritoneal Dialysis
patient.admitDate: The nurse manages peritoneal dialysis exchanges through an abdominal catheter.

labs.r: Slight pink early may be ok.

nursesNote: The nurse manages peritoneal dialysis (PD), which uses the patient's peritoneal membrane as the filter: warmed dialysate is instilled through an abdominal catheter (inflow), allowed to dwell for the ordered time so wastes and fluid move into it, then drained out (outflow). The dialysate is warmed to body temperature with dry heat and never microwaved, which improves comfort and the exchange. Because peritonitis is the major complication, strict aseptic technique is used when connecting the bags. Normal drained effluent is clear and pale yellow; a slight pink tinge can occur in the first few exchanges after catheter placement, but cloudy effluent with abdominal pain and fever is the hallmark of peritonitis and is reported, with a sample sent for culture. Outflow should roughly equal or exceed inflow; sluggish drainage is troubleshooted by repositioning the patient, checking for tubing kinks, keeping the drain bag below the abdomen, and treating constipation. The nurse monitors daily weight, intake and output, and blood glucose (the dextrose in dialysate can raise it), and assesses the catheter exit site for redness or drainage.
providerOrders: Warm the dialysate to body temperature using dry heat
Use strict aseptic technique to connect the dialysis bags
Instill, dwell, and drain per the ordered volumes and times
Inspect and document the effluent; report cloudy returns
Monitor daily weight, intake/output, and blood glucose

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about peritoneal dialysis?

opts.text: PD uses the peritoneal membrane to filter the blood
The dialysate dwells in the abdomen, then is drained out
The dialysate is warmed to body temperature before instilling
The dialysate should be heated in a microwave
Normal drained effluent is clear and pale yellow
Cloudy effluent is a normal expected finding
Warming the dialysate improves comfort and the exchange
Strict aseptic technique prevents peritonitis
Cloudy effluent is the hallmark sign of peritonitis
Outflow much less than inflow may mean a kink or constipation
Bloody effluent days into therapy is always normal
Dextrose in the dialysate can raise blood glucose
Warm the dialysate and connect using aseptic technique
Instill the prescribed dialysate volume (inflow)
Allow the solution to dwell for the ordered time
Drain the fluid and inspect the effluent (outflow)
Measure inflow/outflow and document the effluent

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Expected finding or notify the provider?

opts.text: Clear, pale-yellow effluent
Cloudy effluent with abdominal pain and fever
Outflow roughly equal to or greater than inflow
Slight pink-tinged effluent in the first few exchanges
Markedly reduced drainage with a distended abdomen
Redness and purulent drainage at the exit site
Effluent that returns warm and free-flowing
New fever with rebound abdominal tenderness

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: The drained effluent is cloudy and the patient has abdominal pain and fever
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess the patient and obtain vital signs
Obtain an effluent sample for culture as ordered
Notify the provider of suspected peritonitis
Administer prescribed antibiotics
Document the findings
Warming the dialysate to body temperature
Heating the dialysate in a microwave
Using sterile technique to connect the bags
Treating cloudy effluent as a normal finding
Repositioning the patient when outflow is sluggish
Keeping the drain bag above the abdomen during outflow
Recording inflow, outflow, and effluent appearance
Leaving constipation untreated despite poor drainage

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Reduction of Risk Potential, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
